As before, each abstract contributes a single observation to the annual aggregate

```{r}
# Creating the subcorpus of sentences that explicitly mention AI-related terms 
# Base sentences from AI-related abstracts
sentences_base <- ai_abstracts |>
  unnest_tokens(sentence, abstract, token = "sentences", drop = FALSE) |>
  group_by(id) |>
  mutate(sentence_id = row_number()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(id, publication_year, sentence_id, sentence, abstract)

# Unigram in sentences
sentence_hits_unigrams <- sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence, token = "words") |>
  filter(word %in% ai_lexicon_unigrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Bigram in sentences
sentence_hits_bigrams <- sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(bigram, sentence, token = "ngrams", n = 2) |>
  filter(!is.na(bigram)) |>
  filter(bigram %in% ai_lexicon_bigrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Trigram in sentences
sentence_hits_trigrams <- sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(trigram, sentence, token = "ngrams", n = 3) |>
  filter(!is.na(trigram)) |>
  filter(trigram %in% ai_lexicon_trigrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Combining all sentence-level AI n-grams 
sentence_ai_hits <- bind_rows(
  sentence_hits_unigrams,
  sentence_hits_bigrams,
  sentence_hits_trigrams) |>
  distinct(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Keeping the sentences that explicitly mention AI-related terms
ai_sentences <- sentences_base |>
  semi_join(sentence_ai_hits, by = c("id", "publication_year", "sentence_id"))

ai_sentences
```

Following the previous logic, sentences with no matches in the sentiment lexicons are excluded from the sentiment computing step. The total number of sentences with AI terms are 1544, while in Bing the analysis is carried out using 1210 sentences and in AFINN using 1023.

```{r, warning=FALSE}
# We create a sentence-level id 
ai_sentences_ids <- ai_sentences |>
  mutate(sentence_uid = paste(id, sentence_id, sep = "_"))

# Total sentences that mention AI-related terms 
ai_sentences_ids |>
  summarise(n_ai_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))

# Sentences that have at least one word in the lexicon Bing 
ai_sentences_ids |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  summarise(n_scored_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))

# Sentences that have at least one word in the lexicon AFINN  
ai_sentences_ids |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  summarise(n_scored_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))
```

```{r}
# In this chunk we create two tibbles, one per lexicon, that contain the id of the abstract and sentence, the publication year and the sentiment variables related to each lexicon.

# Bing 
bing_sentence_sentiment <- ai_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  count(id, publication_year, sentence_id, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(
    net_sentiment = positive - negative,
    total_sentiment_words = positive + negative,
    net_sentiment_norm = if_else(
      total_sentiment_words > 0,
      (positive - negative) / total_sentiment_words,
      NA_real_))
bing_sentence_sentiment

# AFINN
afinn_sentence_sentiment <- ai_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(id, publication_year, sentence_id) |>
  summarise(
    afinn_sum = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    afinn_mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_sentiment_words = n(),
    .groups = "drop")
afinn_sentence_sentiment
```

```{r}
# Aggregating sentence-level sentiment to abstract level

# BING: average sentence sentiment within each abstract
bing_sentence_to_abstract <- bing_sentence_sentiment |>
  group_by(id, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    sentiment_score = mean(net_sentiment_norm, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_ai_sentences = n(),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(lexicon = "Bing")

# AFINN: average sentence sentiment within each abstract
afinn_sentence_to_abstract <- afinn_sentence_sentiment |>
  group_by(id, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    sentiment_score = mean(afinn_mean, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_ai_sentences = n(),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(lexicon = "AFINN")

# Combine both lexicons into one single tibble 
sentence_abstract_scores <- bind_rows(
  bing_sentence_to_abstract,
  afinn_sentence_to_abstract)

# Calculating the annual average sentiment by lexicon 
sentence_abstract_yearly <- sentence_abstract_scores |>
  group_by(lexicon, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    mean_sentiment = mean(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_abstracts = n(),
    .groups = "drop")
```

```{r}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

# Plotting the evolution of the annual average sentiment score (using sentences): line chart 
ggplot(sentence_abstract_yearly, aes(x = publication_year, y = mean_sentiment, color = lexicon)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 2016:2025,
    labels = 2016:2025
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Bing" = "#2C3E6B", "AFINN" = "#8E44AD")) +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment in AI-related abstracts over time",
    subtitle = "Yearly mean sentiment per abstract based on AI-mentioning sentences only",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Mean sentiment score",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    legend.position      = "top",
    legend.text          = element_text(size = 9, color = "#4a4a6a"),
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank())

# Plotting the comparison before-after the rise of AI of the annual avergae sentiment score (using sentences): boxplot 

sentence_period_scores <- sentence_abstract_scores |>
  mutate(
    period = if_else(
      publication_year < 2023,
      "Before rise of AI",
      "After rise of AI"))

sentence_period <- sentence_period_scores |>
  group_by(lexicon, period) |>
  summarise(
    mean_sentiment = mean(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_sentiment = median(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_abstracts = n(),
    .groups = "drop")

ggplot(
  sentence_period_scores,
  aes(x = period, y = sentiment_score, fill = period)
) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6, outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Before rise of AI" = "steelblue",
    "After rise of AI"  = "#2C3E6B"
  )) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicon, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment in AI-related abstracts before and after generative AI",
    subtitle = "Based on AI-mentioning sentences only",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Sentiment score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    legend.position      = "none",
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    strip.text           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#1a1a2e"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

# Plotting the number of positive, negative, and neutral AI-related abstracts by year (using sentences)

# Bing
bing_sentence_year_polarity <- bing_sentence_to_abstract |>
  mutate(
    polarity = case_when(
      sentiment_score > 0 ~ "Positive",
      sentiment_score < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ "Neutral")) |>
  count(publication_year, polarity) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "Bing")

# AFINN
afinn_sentence_year_polarity <- afinn_sentence_to_abstract |>
  mutate(
    polarity = case_when(
      sentiment_score > 0 ~ "Positive",
      sentiment_score < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ "Neutral")) |>
  count(publication_year, polarity) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "AFINN")

# Combine both lexicons
sentence_year_polarity <- bind_rows(
  bing_sentence_year_polarity,
  afinn_sentence_year_polarity)

ggplot(
  sentence_year_polarity,
  aes(x = factor(publication_year), y = n, fill = polarity)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Positive" = "#27AE60",
    "Negative" = "#C0392B",
    "Neutral"  = "#2C3E6B"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicon, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of positive, negative, and neutral AI-related abstracts by year",
    subtitle = "Based on AI-mentioning sentences only",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of abstracts",
    fill = "Overall sentiment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    strip.text           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#1a1a2e"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    legend.position      = "bottom",
    legend.text          = element_text(size = 9, color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y   = element_blank()
  )
```

### 5.3. Towards a more target-specific measure of sentiment around AI

The final stage of the analysis moves towards a more target-specific measure of sentiment around AI, trying to identify sentences in which AI is explicitly linked to consequences, risks, benefits, implications, or evaluations. To do this, the code builds a new lexicon by combining AI N-grams with effect and outcome terms: we defined separate vectors for AI-side components and effect-side components, and these were combined into bigrams, trigrams, and fourgrams. After that, the same sentiment analysis procedure is applied: scoring target-specific sentences with Bing and AFINN, aggregating them back to the abstract level, calculating yearly means, comparing positive and negative counts, and examining before/after differences around 2023.

```{r}
# Building the AI-related N-grams lexicon 
# AI SIDE COMPONENT VECTORS

# 1-word AI terms (drawn from ai_lexicon_unigrams)
ai_side_1w <- c(
  # neural architecture terms
  "autoencoder", "perceptron", "neurocomputing",
  # specific AI systems and acronyms
  "chatgpt", "llm",
  # vendors and umbrella terms
  "deepseek", "openai", "ai"
)

# 2-word AI terms (drawn from ai_lexicon_bigrams)
ai_side_2w <- c(
  # core ML / DL paradigms
  "artificial intelligence", "machine learning", "deep learning",
  "reinforcement learning", "machine intelligence", "computational intelligence",
  # network and model architectures
  "neural network", "neural model", "deep network",
  "learning algorithm", "backpropagation learning", "backpropagation algorithm",
  "pretrained transformer",
  # generative and language model terms
  "generative ai", "language model", "agentic ai",
  # AI-generated content
  "ai-generated content",
  # specific systems and vendors
  "openai gpt",
  "claude sonnet", "claude opus", "claude haiku",
  "google gemini", "gemini pro", "gemini ultra",
  "microsoft copilot", "github copilot",
  "mistral ai", "meta llama", "llama 2", "llama 3",
  "stable diffusion",
  "gpt 1", "gpt 2", "gpt 3", "gpt 4", "gpt 5",
  "dall e"
)

# 3-word AI terms (drawn from ai_lexicon_trigrams)
ai_side_3w <- c(
  # large / generative language model formulations
  "large language model", "generative language model",
  "generative large language", "generative pretrained transformer",
  # generative AI formulations
  "generative artificial intelligence", "artificial intelligence generated",
  # OpenAI-specific phrases
  "openai large language", "openai language model",
  # AI + language model phrases
  "ai language model", "ai generated content"
)

# EFFECT / OUTCOME SIDE COMPONENT VECTORS

# 1-word effect and outcome terms
effect_side_1w <- c(
  # direct outcome terms (neutral)
  "effects", "impacts", "consequences", "implications", "outcomes",
  "influence", "affect", "repercussions", "ramifications", "externalities",
  "spillovers", "footprint",

  # valence terms — NEGATIVE (~37)
  "risks", "harms", "dangers", "threats", "hazards", "misuse", "abuse",
  "drawbacks", "disadvantages", "limitations", "failures", "errors",
  "vulnerabilities", "pitfalls", "toxicity", "hallucinations", "unreliability",
  "inaccuracy", "overreliance", "dependence", "dependency", "addiction",
  "misalignment", "deception", "manipulation", "exploitation", "disruption",
  "fragility", "brittleness", "opacity", "obfuscation", "misinformation",
  "disinformation", "propaganda", "plagiarism", "cheating", "displacement",

  # valence terms — POSITIVE (~37)
  "benefits", "opportunities", "advantages", "gains", "improvements",
  "efficiency", "productivity", "innovation", "augmentation", "empowerment",
  "democratization", "accessibility", "scalability", "sustainability",
  "breakthroughs", "capabilities", "accuracy", "reliability", "performance",
  "personalization", "optimization", "collaboration", "assistance",
  "enhancement", "acceleration", "advancement", "progress", "discovery",
  "creation", "support", "convenience", "precision", "explainability",
  "interpretability", "quality", "usefulness", "enablement",

  # neutral evaluative terms
  "challenges", "tradeoffs", "tensions", "paradoxes", "dilemmas",
  "uncertainties", "ambiguities",

  # societal and structural dimensions (neutral descriptors)
  "ethical", "social", "economic", "labor", "employment", "unemployment",
  "inequality", "bias", "fairness", "privacy", "security", "trust",
  "accountability", "transparency", "justice", "discrimination", "welfare",
  "autonomy", "dignity", "democracy", "sovereignty", "marginalization",
  "exclusion", "inclusion", "diversity", "racism", "sexism", "alienation",
  "dehumanization", "commodification", "precarity", "polarization",
  "radicalization", "deskilling", "upskilling", "reskilling", "cohesion",
  "fragmentation", "concentration", "monopolization",

  # environmental dimensions
  "emissions", "carbon", "consumption",

  # health dimensions
  "wellbeing", "health", "safety", "diagnosis", "treatment", "triage",

  # education dimensions
  "literacy", "education", "creativity", "learning",

  # governance terms
  "regulation", "policy", "governance", "legislation", "oversight",
  "compliance", "liability", "audit", "certification", "standards",
  "enforcement", "prohibition", "moratorium", "ban", "restriction",
  "responsibility", "stewardship",

  # human response terms
  "adoption", "acceptance", "resistance", "perception", "attitudes",
  "concerns", "fears", "anxiety", "skepticism", "awareness", "distrust",
  "mistrust", "optimism", "pessimism", "enthusiasm", "reliance",
  "reluctance", "readiness", "preparedness",

  # domain-specific effect nouns
  "automation", "surveillance", "deepfakes", "decision-making"
)

# 2-word effect phrases (for fourgram Pattern B: [AI 2w] + [effect 2w])
effect_side_2w <- c(
  # labour and economic effects — NEGATIVE
  "job displacement", "job loss", "labor disruption", "wage inequality",
  "income inequality", "wage stagnation", "skill displacement", "skill gap",
  "workforce disruption", "technological unemployment", "economic disruption",
  "market concentration", "power concentration",

  # labour and economic effects — POSITIVE
  "job creation", "economic growth", "productivity gains", "wage growth",
  "skill development", "workforce augmentation", "workforce transformation",
  "economic efficiency", "creative destruction", "market competition",
  "value creation",

  # societal and structural effects — NEGATIVE
  "social inequality", "social fragmentation", "digital divide",
  "political polarization", "information disorder", "democratic erosion",
  "civil rights violations",

  # societal and structural effects — POSITIVE
  "social cohesion", "social welfare", "human rights", "civil liberties",
  "democratic participation", "civic engagement", "social mobility",
  "community empowerment",

  # ethical and governance effects — NEGATIVE
  "ethical violations", "ethical concerns", "ethical challenges",
  "ethical dilemmas", "moral hazard", "regulatory failure", "governance gap",

  # ethical and governance effects — POSITIVE
  "ethical guidelines", "ethical frameworks", "policy innovation",
  "regulatory framework", "governance framework", "human oversight",
  "responsible innovation", "responsible development",

  # safety, trust, and reliability — NEGATIVE
  "privacy concerns", "security risks", "data breaches", "system failures",
  "algorithmic bias", "algorithmic discrimination", "automated discrimination",
  "predictive policing", "privacy erosion",

  # safety, trust, and reliability — POSITIVE
  "public trust", "data privacy", "data security", "information security",
  "system reliability", "algorithmic fairness", "algorithmic accountability",
  "algorithmic transparency",

  # health and wellbeing — NEGATIVE
  "mental health risks", "patient harm", "clinical errors",
  "psychological distress", "cognitive overload",

  # health and wellbeing — POSITIVE
  "mental health", "public health", "health outcomes", "patient safety",
  "clinical decision", "early detection", "disease prevention",
  "drug discovery", "personalized medicine",

  # information and media — NEGATIVE
  "fake news", "misinformation spread", "disinformation campaign",
  "information manipulation", "academic dishonesty",

  # information and media — POSITIVE
  "content moderation", "fact checking", "information access",
  "knowledge dissemination", "scientific communication",

  # environmental — NEGATIVE
  "energy consumption", "carbon footprint", "resource consumption",
  "environmental degradation",

  # environmental — POSITIVE
  "energy efficiency", "climate solutions", "resource efficiency",
  "environmental monitoring",

  # decision and justice — NEGATIVE
  "criminal justice bias", "unfair treatment", "rights violations",
  "mass surveillance", "social control",

  # decision and justice — POSITIVE
  "decision making", "criminal justice", "human dignity",
  "fundamental rights", "equal treatment", "evidence-based decision",

  # existential and systemic — NEGATIVE
  "existential risk", "catastrophic risk", "systemic risk",
  "long-term risk", "unintended consequences",

  # existential and systemic — POSITIVE
  "long-term benefits", "systemic improvements", "transformative potential",
  "future opportunities",

  # adoption and use — NEGATIVE
  "misuse potential", "unsafe deployment", "irresponsible use",

  # adoption and use — POSITIVE
  "responsible use", "safe use", "ethical deployment",
  "beneficial application"
)

# 2-word effect-leading prefixes (for trigram Pattern B & fourgram Pattern A)
effect_side_2w_pre <- c(
  # outcome-of constructions (neutral)
  "effects of", "impact of", "impacts of", "consequences of",
  "implications of", "outcomes of", "influence of", "repercussions of",
  "ramifications of", "externalities of",

  # valence-of constructions — NEGATIVE
  "risks of", "harms of", "dangers of", "threats of", "hazards of",
  "misuse of", "abuse of", "limitations of", "failures of", "pitfalls of",
  "disruption by", "disruption from", "downsides of", "costs of",

  # valence-of constructions — POSITIVE
  "benefits of", "opportunities of", "advantages of", "potential of",
  "promise of", "gains from", "strengths of", "value of",
  "utility of", "contributions of", "advances in", "progress in",

  # neutral evaluative constructions
  "challenges of", "tradeoffs of", "concerns about", "debates about",
  "discourse on",

  # governance and responsibility (neutral)
  "governance of", "regulation of", "oversight of", "ethics of",
  "accountability for", "responsibility for", "liability for",
  "standards for", "audit of",

  # adoption and use (neutral)
  "adoption of", "use of", "deployment of", "integration of",
  "reliance on", "dependence on",

  # trust and perception — NEGATIVE
  "distrust of", "mistrust of", "fears about", "anxiety about",
  "skepticism about", "resistance to",

  # trust and perception — POSITIVE
  "trust in", "optimism about", "enthusiasm about",
  "attitudes toward", "attitudes towards", "perceptions of", "acceptance of",
  "awareness of",

  # threat constructions — NEGATIVE
  "threats to", "dangers to", "harm from", "damage from", "risks to",

  # opportunity constructions — POSITIVE
  "hope for", "support for", "case for",

  # role constructions (neutral)
  "role of", "power of"
)

# 3-word effect-leading prefixes (for fourgram Pattern C: [effect prefix 3w] + [AI 1w])
effect_side_3w_pre <- c(
  # ethical / social / economic implications of — NEGATIVE
  "ethical implications of", "social implications of",
  "economic implications of", "societal implications of",
  "political implications of", "legal implications of",
  "ethical challenges of", "social challenges of", "economic challenges of",
  "ethical concerns about", "social concerns about", "safety concerns about",

  # ethical / social / economic implications of — POSITIVE
  "ethical opportunities of", "social benefits of",
  "economic benefits of", "societal benefits of",
  "transformative potential of", "positive implications of",
  "innovative potential of",

  # impact on domain constructions — NEGATIVE
  "impact on inequality", "impact on privacy", "impact on surveillance",
  "impact on misinformation",

  # impact on domain constructions — POSITIVE
  "impact on society", "impact on democracy", "impact on employment",
  "impact on healthcare", "impact on education", "impact on labor",
  "impact on security", "impact on creativity", "impact on wellbeing",
  "impact on journalism", "impact on science", "impact on research",
  "impact on productivity",

  # social / economic / societal impact of — NEGATIVE
  "negative impact of", "harmful effects of", "adverse impact of",

  # social / economic / societal impact of — POSITIVE
  "social impact of", "economic impact of", "societal impact of",
  "environmental impact of", "positive impact of", "beneficial impact of",

  # public and societal framing — NEGATIVE
  "societal risks of", "societal harms of",

  # public and societal framing — POSITIVE
  "public perception of", "societal effects of", "societal benefits of",
  "societal contributions of",

  # risk / benefit framing (inherently balanced pairs)
  "risks and benefits", "benefits and risks", "risks and opportunities",
  "threats and opportunities", "costs and benefits",
  "risks associated with", "effects associated with",
  "impact associated with", "challenges associated with",
  "opportunities associated with", "concerns associated with",
  "harms associated with", "benefits associated with",
  "dangers associated with", "implications associated with",

  # potential framing — NEGATIVE
  "potential risks of", "potential harms of", "potential dangers of",

  # potential framing — POSITIVE
  "potential benefits of", "potential impact of", "potential effects of",
  "potential implications of", "potential value of",

  # responsible / ethical use framing — NEGATIVE
  "misuse and abuse", "unsafe deployment of",

  # responsible / ethical use framing — POSITIVE
  "responsible use of", "ethical use of", "safe use of",
  "responsible deployment of", "beneficial use of",

  # governance and policy framing (neutral)
  "governance and regulation", "policy implications of",
  "regulation and governance", "ethical governance of",
  "regulatory challenges of", "policy challenges of",

  # bias / fairness / privacy framing (neutral)
  "bias and fairness", "bias and discrimination",
  "privacy and security", "privacy and surveillance",
  "fairness and accountability", "transparency and accountability",
  "trust and accountability", "safety and security",

  # threat and opportunity framing — NEGATIVE
  "threats posed by", "risks posed by", "dangers posed by",
  "harms caused by", "disruption caused by",

  # threat and opportunity framing — POSITIVE
  "opportunities presented by", "benefits offered by",
  "value created by", "progress enabled by",

  # labour and displacement framing — NEGATIVE
  "labor market disruption", "displacement caused by",

  # labour and displacement framing — POSITIVE
  "labor market effects", "labor market impact", "labor market consequences",
  "automation driven by", "growth driven by",

  # temporal framing — NEGATIVE
  "long-term risks of", "short-term risks of",

  # temporal framing — POSITIVE
  "long-term effects of", "short-term effects of",
  "long-term impact of", "short-term impact of",
  "long-term benefits of", "long-term implications of",

  # unintended / adverse vs. intended / positive framing
  "unintended consequences of", "adverse effects of",
  "negative consequences of", "negative effects of",
  "positive effects of", "positive impact of",
  "intended benefits of",

  # public debate and discourse framing (neutral)
  "public debate on", "public discourse on",
  "debate about the", "concerns regarding the",
  "questions about the"
)

# COMBINATION STEPS

# ~1,800 bigrams
# [AI 1w] + [effect 1w]  AND  [effect 1w] + [AI 1w]
ai_sentiment_bigrams <- unique(trimws(c(
  as.vector(outer(ai_side_1w,     effect_side_1w, paste)),
  as.vector(outer(effect_side_1w, ai_side_1w,     paste))
)))

# ~5,400 trigrams
# Pattern A: [AI 2w]            + [effect 1w]
# Pattern B: [effect prefix 2w] + [AI 1w]
trigrams_a <- as.vector(outer(ai_side_2w,         effect_side_1w, paste))
trigrams_b <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_2w_pre, ai_side_1w,     paste))
ai_sentiment_trigrams <- unique(trimws(c(trigrams_a, trigrams_b)))

# ~10,200 fourgrams
# Pattern A: [effect prefix 2w] + [AI 2w]    — e.g. "risks of machine learning"
# Pattern B: [AI 2w]            + [effect 2w] — e.g. "machine learning job displacement"
# Pattern C: [effect prefix 3w] + [AI 1w]    — e.g. "ethical implications of ai"
# Pattern D: [AI 3w]            + [effect 1w] — e.g. "large language model risks"
fourgrams_a <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_2w_pre, ai_side_2w,    paste))
fourgrams_b <- as.vector(outer(ai_side_2w,         effect_side_2w, paste))
fourgrams_c <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_3w_pre, ai_side_1w,    paste))
fourgrams_d <- as.vector(outer(ai_side_3w,         effect_side_1w, paste))
ai_sentiment_fourgrams <- unique(trimws(c(fourgrams_a, fourgrams_b, fourgrams_c, fourgrams_d)))
```

```{r}
# Creating the subcorpus of sentences that explicitly talk about AI  
# Base sentences from AI-related abstracts
target_sentences_base <- ai_abstracts |>
  unnest_tokens(sentence, abstract, token = "sentences", drop = FALSE) |>
  group_by(id) |>
  mutate(sentence_id = row_number()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(id, publication_year, sentence_id, sentence, abstract)

# Bigram hits in sentences
target_hits_bigrams <- target_sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(bigram, sentence, token = "ngrams", n = 2) |>
  filter(!is.na(bigram)) |>
  filter(bigram %in% ai_sentiment_bigrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Trigram hits in sentences
target_hits_trigrams <- target_sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(trigram, sentence, token = "ngrams", n = 3) |>
  filter(!is.na(trigram)) |>
  filter(trigram %in% ai_sentiment_trigrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Fourgram hits in sentences
target_hits_fourgrams <- target_sentences_base |>
  unnest_tokens(fourgram, sentence, token = "ngrams", n = 4) |>
  filter(!is.na(fourgram)) |>
  filter(fourgram %in% ai_sentiment_fourgrams) |>
  transmute(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Combining all target-specific hits
target_sentence_hits <- bind_rows(
  target_hits_bigrams,
  target_hits_trigrams,
  target_hits_fourgrams
) |>
  distinct(id, publication_year, sentence_id)

# Keeping only target-specific sentences
ai_target_sentences <- target_sentences_base |>
  semi_join(target_sentence_hits, by = c("id", "publication_year", "sentence_id"))

ai_target_sentences
```

This target-specific filtering step reduces drastically the number of selected sentences to 266. The Bing analysis is carried out using 212 sentences and in AFINN using 190, which may limit the generalization of the results.

```{r}
# We create a sentence-level id 
ai_target_sentences_ids <- ai_target_sentences |>
  mutate(sentence_uid = paste(id, sentence_id, sep = "_"))

# Total sentences that are target-specific
ai_target_sentences_ids |>
  summarise(n_ai_target_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))

# Target-specific sentences that have at least one word in the lexicon Bing 
ai_target_sentences_ids |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  summarise(n_bing_scored_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))

# Target-specific sentences that have at least one word in the lexicon AFINN 
ai_target_sentences_ids |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  summarise(n_afinn_scored_sentences = n_distinct(sentence_uid))
```

```{r}
# In this chunk we create two tibbles, one per lexicon, that contain the id of the abstract and sentence, the publication year and the sentiment variables related to each lexicon.

# Bing sentiment by target-specific sentence
bing_target_sentence_sentiment <- ai_target_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  count(id, publication_year, sentence_id, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(
    net_sentiment = positive - negative,
    total_sentiment_words = positive + negative,
    net_sentiment_norm = if_else(
      total_sentiment_words > 0,
      (positive - negative) / total_sentiment_words,
      NA_real_))

bing_target_sentence_sentiment

# AFINN sentiment by target-specific sentence
afinn_target_sentence_sentiment <- ai_target_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(id, publication_year, sentence_id) |>
  summarise(
    afinn_sum = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    afinn_mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_sentiment_words = n(),
    .groups = "drop")

afinn_target_sentence_sentiment
```

```{r}
# Target-specific sentence-level sentiment aggregated back to abstract level

# BING: average target-specific sentence sentiment within each abstract
bing_target_sentence_to_abstract <- bing_target_sentence_sentiment |>
  group_by(id, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    sentiment_score = mean(net_sentiment_norm, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_target_sentences = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "Bing")

# AFINN: average target-specific sentence sentiment within each abstract
afinn_target_sentence_to_abstract <- afinn_target_sentence_sentiment |>
  group_by(id, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    sentiment_score = mean(afinn_mean, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_target_sentences = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "AFINN")

# Combining both lexicons
target_sentence_abstract_scores <- bind_rows(
  bing_target_sentence_to_abstract,
  afinn_target_sentence_to_abstract)

# Calculating the annual average sentiment by lexicon 
target_sentence_abstract_yearly <- target_sentence_abstract_scores |>
  group_by(lexicon, publication_year) |>
  summarise(
    mean_sentiment = mean(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_abstracts = n(),
    .groups = "drop")
```

```{r}
# Plotting the evolution of the annual average sentiment score (using target-specific sentences): line chart 
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

ggplot(
  target_sentence_abstract_yearly,
  aes(x = publication_year, y = mean_sentiment, color = lexicon)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 2016:2025,
    labels = 2016:2025
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Bing" = "#2C3E6B", "AFINN" = "#8E44AD")) +
  labs(
    title = "Target-specific sentiment in AI-related abstracts over time",
    subtitle = "Yearly mean sentiment per abstract based on target-specific AI-effect sentences",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Mean sentiment score",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    legend.position      = "top",
    legend.text          = element_text(size = 9, color = "#4a4a6a"),
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank()
  )

# Plotting the comparison before-after the rise of AI of the annual avergae sentiment score (using target-specific sentences): boxplot 

target_sentence_period_scores <- target_sentence_abstract_scores |>
  mutate(
    period = if_else(
      publication_year < 2023,
      "Before rise of AI",
      "After rise of AI"))

target_sentence_period <- target_sentence_period_scores |>
  group_by(lexicon, period) |>
  summarise(
    mean_sentiment = mean(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_sentiment = median(sentiment_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_abstracts = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  target_sentence_period_scores,
  aes(x = period, y = sentiment_score, fill = period)
) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6, outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Before rise of AI" = "steelblue",
    "After rise of AI"  = "#2C3E6B"
  )) +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicon, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment in AI-related abstracts before and after generative AI",
    subtitle = "Based on target-specific AI-effect sentences only",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Sentiment score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    legend.position      = "none",
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    strip.text           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#1a1a2e"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

# Plotting the number of positive, negative, and neutral AI-related abstracts by year (using target-specific sentences)

# Bing
bing_target_sentence_year_polarity <- bing_target_sentence_to_abstract |>
  mutate(
    polarity = case_when(
      sentiment_score > 0 ~ "Positive",
      sentiment_score < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ "Neutral"
    )
  ) |>
  count(publication_year, polarity) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "Bing")

# AFINN
afinn_target_sentence_year_polarity <- afinn_target_sentence_to_abstract |>
  mutate(
    polarity = case_when(
      sentiment_score > 0 ~ "Positive",
      sentiment_score < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ "Neutral"
    )
  ) |>
  count(publication_year, polarity) |>
  mutate(lexicon = "AFINN")

# Combine both lexicons
target_sentence_year_polarity <- bind_rows(
  bing_target_sentence_year_polarity,
  afinn_target_sentence_year_polarity
)

ggplot(
  target_sentence_year_polarity,
  aes(x = factor(publication_year), y = n, fill = polarity)
) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Positive" = "#27AE60",
    "Negative" = "#C0392B",
    "Neutral"  = "#2C3E6B"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicon, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of positive, negative, and neutral AI-related abstracts by year",
    subtitle = "Based on target-specific AI-effect sentences only",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of abstracts",
    fill = "Overall sentiment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    strip.text           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#1a1a2e"),
    axis.text.x          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y          = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    legend.position      = "bottom",
    legend.text          = element_text(size = 9, color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor     = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y   = element_blank()
  )
```

### 5.4 Which words drive the sentiment in target-specific AI-effect sentences?

Finally, we explore which words drive the sentiment in these target-specific AI-effect sentences of scientific papers' abstracts. We count the frequency of positive and negative words by lexicon within the selected sentences and we can observe agian the pattern that, in both lexicons, positive words contribute more to the overall sentiment than negative ones. On the positive side, words such as trust, ethical and intelligence and verbs like support, enhance and promote appear very often. On the negative side, words like bias, risks, distrust, critical, issues or negative are the most common in both lexicons. This set of terms provide insights into how artificial intelligence is conceptualized in scientific papers and their abstracts, the social risks and methodological limitations it creates, as well as its beneficial role.

```{r}
# We create a data frame with word, sentiment, and number of mentions to see which words are more common

# Bing 
bing_target_word_counts <- ai_target_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE)
bing_target_word_counts

# AFINN 
afinn_target_word_counts <- ai_target_sentences |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  mutate(
    sentiment = case_when(
      value > 0 ~ "Positive",
      value < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ "Neutral")) |>
  filter(sentiment != "Neutral") |>
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE)
afinn_target_word_counts
```

```{r}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

# Plotting the top contributing words by sentiment by lexicon 

target_word_counts <- bind_rows(
  bing_target_word_counts |>
    mutate(
      lexicon = "Bing",
      sentiment = case_when(
        sentiment == "positive" ~ "Positive",
        sentiment == "negative" ~ "Negative",
        TRUE ~ sentiment)),
  afinn_target_word_counts |>
    mutate(lexicon = "AFINN"))

target_word_counts |>
  group_by(lexicon, sentiment) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 15, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(panel = paste(lexicon, sentiment, sep = " - ")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = reorder(word, n), fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ panel, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Positive" = "#27AE60",
    "Negative" = "#C0392B")) +
  labs(
    title = "Words driving sentiment in target-specific AI-effect sentences",
    subtitle = "Top 15 positive and negative words matched by each lexicon",
    x = "Contribution to sentiment",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#1a1a2e"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10, color = "#1a1a2e"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(color = "#4a4a6a"),
    axis.text.y      = element_text(size = 8, color = "#4a4a6a"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )
```



## Section 6. IF WE CAN \[RQ5\]

## Section 7. Conclussions and limitations

## Section 8. References

Ding, L., Lawson, C., & Shapira, P. (2025). Rise of generative artificial intelligence in science. *Scientometrics*, 130(9), 5093–5114. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11192-025-05413-z

Liu, N., Shapira, P., & Yue, X. (2021). Tracking developments in artificial intelligence research: Constructing and applying a new search strategy. *Scientometrics*, 126(4), 3153–3192. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11192-021-03868-4

## Appendix I - Domain specific stopword list prompt

The domain-specific stopword list used in Section 3 was generated with the assistance of a large language model (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Anthropic). The complete prompt is reproduced below.

::: {style="border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 1em; border-radius: 4px;"}
```         
You are an expert computational linguist and text mining specialist with deep 
knowledge of social science research and academic writing conventions. You are 
working on a large-scale corpus analysis project described in the context below.

<project_context>
The project analyzes a corpus of journal abstracts drawn from six leading social 
science journals over the period 2016–2025:

  - Nature Human Behaviour (behavioral science, interdisciplinary)
  - Humanities and Social Sciences Communications (interdisciplinary)
  - American Sociological Review (sociology, North America)
  - American Political Science Review (political science, North America)
  - European Sociological Review (sociology, Europe)
  - European Journal of Political Research (political science, Europe)

The corpus spans the period before and after the public rise of generative AI 
(November 2022, release of ChatGPT). Section 3 of the project (for which these 
stopwords are being prepared) addresses two research questions:

  RQ1: What are the predominant topics in top social science journals, by journal 
       and by year?
  RQ2: Has AI (in the broad sense) become one of the predominant topics, and has 
       it influenced the broader topical landscape?

The unit of analysis is the abstract (short, dense, highly formulaic texts 
averaging 150–250 words), written in academic English.
</project_context>

<corpus_frequency_data>
The file corpus_word_freq.csv is attached. It contains the top words in the corpus 
after removing standard English stopwords  and  numeric tokens, generated directly
from thousands of abstracts using tidytext. Columns:
  - word: the token (lowercase)
  - n: raw frequency count across all abstracts

Use this frequency data as empirical grounding for your stopword list:
  - Words that appear here at high frequency AND carry no thematic/discriminative 
    value are strong candidates for inclusion in the stopword list
  - Do not limit yourself to words in this list — be exhaustive across all 7 
    categories — but treat high-frequency words as a priority checklist to 
    ensure nothing obvious is missed
  - High-frequency words that carry topical signal (per the preservation rules 
    below) must NOT be added to the stopword list even if they rank highly
    (e.g. "policy", "political", "education", "health")
</corpus_frequency_data>

<task>
Generate a comprehensive, categorised list of domain-specific stopwords to be 
removed from this corpus. These are words that:

  (a) carry no thematic or discriminative value for social science research AND
  (b) appear at high frequency across the corpus, AND

Before generating each category, cross-check it against the corpus_frequency_data 
above to ensure all high-frequency uninformative words are captured.

Return the list as a clean, categorised R tibble with a column for the stopword 
and a column for its category, preceded by commented section headers explaining 
the rationale for each category.
</task>

<categories_to_cover>
Generate stopwords across ALL of the following categories. For each category, think 
systematically about what words in that class appear in social science abstracts, words like:

1. ACADEMIC BOILERPLATE VERBS
   Verbs used generically across all abstracts regardless of topic: reporting verbs 
   ("examine", "investigate", "explore", "suggest", "find", "show", "demonstrate", 
   "analyze", "assess", "evaluate", "discuss", "argue", "propose", "address", 
   "consider", "focus", "aim", "seek", "test", "draw", "provide", "offer", 
   "highlight", "reveal", "indicate", "identify", "contribute", "develop"), 
   and their inflected forms where relevant.

2. GENERIC METHODOLOGICAL TERMS
   Words describing research designs, data types, and analytical approaches that 
   are ubiquitous across social science and carry no topical signal: "survey", 
   "questionnaire", "interview", "sample", "dataset",  "longitudinal", "cross-sectional",
   "regression", "model", "framework",  "approach", "method", "methodology",
   "analysis", "data", "measure", "variable", "coefficient", "estimate", "robustness",
   "validity", "reliability", etc.

3. ABSTRACT-STRUCTURAL BOILERPLATE
   Phrases and words used to signal the structure of an abstract itself rather than 
   its content: "paper", "study", "article", "research", "literature", "review", 
   "background", "objective", "purpose", "contribution", "implication", "finding", 
   "result", "conclusion", "evidence", "prior", "existing", "previous", "recent", 
   "current", "present", "based", "using", "used", "given", "among", "across".

4. GENERIC SOCIAL SCIENCE CONCEPTS
   Terms so broadly used in social science that they provide no discriminative 
   topical signal between documents: "society", "social", "individual", "group", 
   "population", "people", "person", "human", "behavior", "behaviour", "attitude", 
   "perception", "factor", "role", "relationship", "impact", "effect", "influence", 
   "context", "level", "process", "system", "structure", "pattern", "change", 
   "difference", "association", "correlation", etc.

5. JOURNAL-SPECIFIC AND PUBLICATION BOILERPLATE
   Words likely introduced by journal formatting, editorial conventions, or abstract 
   templates specific to these six journals: copyright notices, volume/issue markers, 
   DOI fragments, and any words that appear due to systematic metadata leakage.

6. ENCODING ARTEFACTS AND NOISE
   Tokens that result from text extraction, encoding issues, or tokenisation: 
   single characters (except meaningful ones), numeric tokens, garbled unicode 
   fragments, OCR noise, HTML remnants, and other non-linguistic tokens.

7. HIGH-FREQUENCY FUNCTION WORDS NOT CAUGHT BY STANDARD STOPWORD LISTS
   Domain-specific function-like words that standard English stopword lists 
   (e.g., the Snowball or SMART lists) miss but which are uninformative in this 
   corpus: discipline-specific connectives, hedging phrases reduced to single tokens 
   ("however", "furthermore", "moreover", "nevertheless", "thus", "therefore", 
   "hence", "whereas", "whereby", "therein"), and academic filler adjectives 
   ("important", "significant", "key", "major", "critical", "novel", "robust", 
   "comprehensive", "systematic", "rigorous", "substantial").
</categories_to_cover>

<preservation_rules>
CRITICAL: The following word classes must NOT appear in the stopword list, even if 
they are frequent. Removing them would destroy the thematic signal needed for RQ1 
and RQ2:

  - AI and technology terms: any term related to artificial intelligence, machine 
    learning, large language models, specific model names, or related technology. 
    These are central to RQ2 and must be preserved.
  - Substantive social science topic terms: words indexing real research topics 
    such as "inequality", "democracy", "migration", "climate", "health", "labor", 
    "race", "gender", "education", "voting", "polarization", "trust", "identity", 
    "discrimination", "poverty", "welfare", etc.
  - Sentiment-bearing terms: words with clear positive or negative valence that 
    will be needed for the sentiment analysis in Section 5.
  - Proper nouns and named entities: country names, institutional names, author 
    names — these may carry topical signal.
  - Temporally specific terms: words that may index pre/post ChatGPT content, 
    which are needed for the temporal analyses in Sections 3–6.

When in doubt about whether a word carries signal, err on the side of keeping it.
</preservation_rules>

<output_format>
Return only executable R code. Structure it as follows:

  library(tibble)

  domain_stopwords <- tibble(
    word = c(
      # 1. ACADEMIC BOILERPLATE VERBS
      # These verbs appear in virtually every abstract regardless of topic.
      # Removing them prevents them from dominating topic-word distributions
      # in LDA and collapsing IDF weights in TF-IDF.
      "examine", "investigate", ...

      # 2. GENERIC METHODOLOGICAL TERMS
      # ...

      # [continue for all 7 categories]
    ),
    category = c(
      # mirror the word vector exactly, one category label per word
      "boilerplate_verb", "boilerplate_verb", ...
    )
  )

Rules:
  - All stopwords in lowercase
  - One row per word (not per phrase, we operate on unigrams after tokenisation)
  - The word and category vectors must be exactly the same length
  - Category labels should be short snake_case strings matching the 7 categories
  - Add a final comment line reporting the total count: # Total: N stopwords
  - Be exhaustive, put lots of effort
</output_format>
```
:::

## Appendix II - AI effects lexicon prompt

::: {style="border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 1em; border-radius: 4px;"}
```         
You are an expert computational linguist and text mining specialist working on a 
systematic literature review about AI and its effects. Your task is to create three 
R vectors of n-grams that will be used to identify academic journal abstracts addressing artificial intelligence (AI) or its effects, consequences, risks, 
benefits, or impacts.

<objective>
Precision over recall. We accept missing relevant abstracts, but every abstract 
matched by these n-grams must unambiguously be about AI or its effects.
</objective>

<existing_ai_lexicon>
We already have a lexicon that identifies AI-related vocabulary. Use it as your 
reference for how AI is referred to across our corpus. Your component vectors must 
be compatible with this lexicon —meaning the AI-side tokens should draw from these 
terms or their natural variants—.

# AI unigram lexicon
ai_lexicon_unigrams <- c(
  "autoencoder", "perceptron", "neurocomputing",
  "chatgpt", "llm",
  "deepseek", "openai", "ai"
)
# AI bigram lexicon
ai_lexicon_bigrams <- c(
  "artificial intelligence", "neural network", "machine learning",
  "deep learning", "reinforcement learning", "learning algorithm",
  "backpropagation learning", "backpropagation algorithm",
  "machine intelligence", "computational intelligence",
  "neural model", "deep network",
  "generative ai", "language model", "pretrained transformer",
  "ai-generated content", "openai gpt",
  "claude sonnet", "claude opus", "claude haiku",
  "google gemini", "gemini pro", "gemini ultra",
  "microsoft copilot", "github copilot", "mistral ai",
  "meta llama", "llama 2", "llama 3",
  "stable diffusion", "gpt 5", "gpt 4", "gpt 3", "gpt 2", "gpt 1",
  "dall e", "agentic ai"
)
# AI trigram lexicon
ai_lexicon_trigrams <- c(
  "large language model", "generative language model",
  "generative large language", "generative pretrained transformer",
  "generative artificial intelligence", "artificial intelligence generated",
  "openai large language", "openai language model",
  "ai language model", "ai generated content"
)
</existing_ai_lexicon>

<task>
Rather than writing every n-gram by hand, use a **combinatorial approach**: define 
separate component vectors for the AI side and the effects side, then generate the 
final n-gram vectors programmatically using outer() and paste(). This makes the 
lexicon scalable and easy to maintain.
The architecture for each n-gram size is as follows:
## BIGRAMS (2 words total)
Combine:
  - ai_side_1w: AI unigrams (1-word AI terms, e.g. "ai", "llm", "chatgpt")
  - effect_side_1w: effect unigrams (1-word effect/outcome terms, e.g. "risks", 
    "benefits")
Generate with:
  ai_sentiment_bigrams <- unique(trimws(as.vector(
    outer(ai_side_1w, effect_side_1w, paste)
  )))
Note: outer() by default produces [AI term] + [effect term] order. To also capture
[effect term] + [AI term] order (e.g. "risks ai", "benefits llm"), generate both 
directions and combine:
  ai_sentiment_bigrams <- unique(trimws(c(
    as.vector(outer(ai_side_1w, effect_side_1w, paste)),
    as.vector(outer(effect_side_1w, ai_side_1w, paste))
  )))
## TRIGRAMS (3 words total)
Trigrams can be formed from two structural patterns. Generate both and combine:
Pattern A — [2-word AI term] + [1-word effect term]:
  Uses: ai_side_2w (AI bigrams) × effect_side_1w (effect unigrams)
  Example: "machine learning" + "risks" → "machine learning risks"
Pattern B — [2-word effect prefix] + [1-word AI term]:
  Define effect_side_2w_pre as 2-word effect-leading phrases like 
  "effects of", "risks of", "impact of", etc., then combine with ai_side_1w:
  Example: "effects of" + "ai" → "effects of ai"
  effect_side_2w_pre <- c("effects of", "impact of", "risks of", ...)
  
  # Pattern A: [2-word AI term] + [1-word effect term]
  trigrams_a <- as.vector(outer(ai_side_2w, effect_side_1w, paste))
  
  # Pattern B: [2-word effect prefix] + [1-word AI term]
  trigrams_b <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_2w_pre, ai_side_1w, paste))
  
  ai_sentiment_trigrams <- unique(trimws(c(trigrams_a, trigrams_b)))
  
## FOURGRAMS (4 words total)
Fourgrams can be formed from three structural patterns. Generate all and combine:
Pattern A — [2-word effect prefix] + [2-word AI term]:
  effect_side_2w_pre × ai_side_2w
  Example: "effects of" + "machine learning" → "effects of machine learning"
Pattern B — [2-word AI term] + [2-word effect term]:
  ai_side_2w × effect_side_2w
  Example: "machine learning" + "job displacement" → 
           "machine learning job displacement"
Pattern C — [3-word effect prefix] + [1-word AI term]:
  effect_side_3w_pre × ai_side_1w
  Example: "ethical implications of" + "ai" → "ethical implications of ai"
  fourgrams_a <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_2w_pre, ai_side_2w, paste))
  fourgrams_b <- as.vector(outer(ai_side_2w, effect_side_2w, paste))
  fourgrams_c <- as.vector(outer(effect_side_3w_pre, ai_side_1w, paste))
  ai_sentiment_fourgrams <- unique(trimws(c(fourgrams_a, fourgrams_b, fourgrams_c)))
</task>

<component_vector_requirements>
Define all component vectors clearly before the combination step, with inline 
comments grouping terms by semantic category. All strings must be lowercase.
The effects/outcome side vectors must cover a broad semantic field, including:
- Direct outcome terms: effects, impacts, consequences, implications, outcomes, 
  influence
- Valence terms: benefits, risks, harms, dangers, threats, opportunities, 
  advantages, disadvantages, challenges (IMPORTANT: make sure that non-neutral terms are balanced, that is, make sure there is no bias towards "positive" or "negative" valence)
- Societal dimensions: ethical, social, economic, labor, employment, inequality, 
  bias, fairness, privacy, security, trust, accountability, transparency
- Governance terms: regulation, policy, governance, legislation, oversight
- Human response terms: adoption, acceptance, resistance, perception, attitudes, 
  concerns, fears
- Domain-specific effect nouns: automation, misinformation, discrimination, 
  creativity, decision-making, displacement, surveillance
Use ALL terms from the existing lexicon on the AI side —include every term across 
unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams without exception—.
</component_vector_requirements>

<precision_rules>
The only constraint on generated combinations is semantic unambiguity: if a 
combination appears in an abstract, it must mean that AI or its effects addressed. Whether a combination actually occurs in the corpus is irrelevant —unmatched n-grams are harmless—. Go for exhaustive coverage. Do not prune for 
plausibility. 
Apply unique() and trimws() to deduplicate and clean whitespace.
</precision_rules>

<output_format>
Return only executable R code. Structure it in this order:
1. All component vectors (with inline comments by semantic category)
2. The three combination steps producing ai_sentiment_bigrams, 
   ai_sentiment_trigrams, ai_sentiment_fourgrams
3. A brief comment above each final vector stating how many n-grams 
   it contains, e.g.: # ~120 bigrams
No prose before or after the code block.
</output_format>
```
:::

## Appendix III - Unigram TF-IDF context windows

Some of the terms in the TF-IDF results were unclear: they were abbreviations or unexpected terms with no immediately evident interpretation. In order to understand them better, we explored their context windows using the `kwic` function from the `quanteda` package. We include them here for transparency and to allow the reader to verify our interpretations.

```{r}
# Create the corpus, linking text to our ID column
abstracts_corpus <- corpus(articles,
                           text_field = "abstract",
                           docid_field = "id")
```


```{r}
# Year 2016
# kwic = 400th
# We specify `quanteda::tokens` because there are conflicts with other libraries
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "400th", window = 8)
# kwic = geg
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "geg", window = 8)
# kwic = mlg
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "mlg", window = 8)

# Year 2017
# kwic = gothic
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "gothic", window = 8)
# kwic = fia
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "fia", window = 8)

# Year 2018
# kwic = graffiti
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "graffiti", window = 8)
# kwic = revok
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "revok", window = 8)
# kwic = tves
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "tves", window = 8)
# kwic = gamh
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "gamh", window = 8)
# kwic = nua
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "nua", window = 8)

# Year 2019
# kwic = gun
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "gun", window = 8)
# kwic = guns
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "guns", window = 8)
# kwic = lgds
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "lgds", window = 8)
# kwic = ccs
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "ccs", window = 8)

# Year 2020
# kwic = pea
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "pea", window = 6)
# kwic = mta
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "mta", window = 6)
# kwic = silcrete
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "silcrete", window = 8)

# Year 2021
# kwic = coconut
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "coconut", window = 8)
# kwic = hcws
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "hcws", window = 6)
# kwic = wc
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "wc", window = 6)

# Year 2023
# kwic = csr
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "csr", window = 6)
# kwic = dms
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "dms", window = 6)
# kwic = smmes
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "smmes", window = 6)

# Year 2024-2025
# kwic = esg
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "esg", window = 6)
# kwic = efl
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "efl", window = 6)
# kwic = fintech
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), "fintech", window = 6)
```

## Appendix IV - Bigram TF-IDF context windows

Some of the terms in the TF-IDF results were unclear: they were abbreviations or unexpected terms with no immediately evident interpretation. In order to understand them better, we explored their context windows using the `kwic` function from the `quanteda` package. We include them here for transparency and to allow the reader to verify our interpretations.

```{r}
# Year 2016
# kwic = european sociological (this is metadata that we cannot filter without
# potentially losing relevant information, since the bigram is composed by
# two terms that could be meaninful in other combinations)
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("european sociological"), window = 8)

# Year 2017
# kwic = social pact
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("social pact"), window = 8)
# kwic = veto player
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("veto player"), window = 8)

# Year 2018
# kwic = mind upload
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("mind upload"), window = 8)
# kwic = fairy tale
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("fairy tale"), window = 8)

# Year 2020
# kwic = science diplomacy
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("science diplomacy"), window = 8)
# kwic = grass pea
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("grass pea"), window = 8)
# kwic = gun culture
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("gun culture"), window = 8)
# kwic = agile sd
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("agile sd"), window = 8)
# kwic = uhi tools
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("uhi tools"), window = 8)
# kwic = sa mta
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("sa mta"), window = 8)

# Year 2021
# kwic = frontline hcws
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("frontline hcws"), window = 8)

# Year 2023
# kwic = burden reduction
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("burden reduction"), window = 8)

# Year 2025
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("carbon emissions"), window = 8)
```


## Appendix V - Trigram TF-IDF context windows

Some of the terms in the TF-IDF results were unclear: they were abbreviations or unexpected terms with no immediately evident interpretation. In order to understand them better, we explored their context windows using the `kwic` function from the `quanteda` package. We include them here for transparency and to allow the reader to verify our interpretations.

```{r}
# Year 2016
# kwic = balance lifestyle mental
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("balance lifestyle mental"), window = 8)

# Year 2017
# kwic = veto player theory
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("veto player theory"), window = 8)

# Year 2018
# kwic = search query data
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("search query data"), window = 8)
# kwic = axial age concept
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("axial age concept"), window = 8)

# Year 2019
# kwic = eclipse prediction scheme
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("eclipse prediction scheme"), window = 8)
# kwic = zika conspiracy theories
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("zika conspiracy theories"), window = 8)

# Year 2020
# kwic = grass pea production
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("grass pea production"), window = 8)
# kwic = agile sd implementation
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("agile sd implementation"), window = 8)
# kwic = probability ratio index
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("probability ratio index"), window = 8)

# Year 2021
# kwic = dravidian tooth word
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("dravidian tooth word"), window = 8)
# kwic = proto dravidian tooth
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("proto dravidian tooth"), window = 8)
# kwic = av pilot projects
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("av pilot projects"), window = 8)
# kwic = short run sustainability
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("short run sustainability"), window = 8)

# Year 2022
# kwic = golf simulation industry
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("golf simulation industry"), window = 8)
# kwic = significantly lower sle
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("significantly lower sle"), window = 8)

# Year 2024
# kwic = squares structural equation
kwic(quanteda::tokens(abstracts_corpus), phrase("squares structural equation"), window = 8)
```
